summary: Adversarial red team against the climate-disclosure service line plan: strategy, pricing, GTM, build architecture

kills.a: The white-label channel's core economic claim is non-transferable. Under APES GN 30 and ASQM 1, a firm using an outsourced service provider retains primary professional responsibility for the work and must run quality-management controls over it. If my letterhead goes on an AASB S2 evidence pack and draft statements, my partners must supervise, review and be able to defend every classification, calc run and drafted sentence, because when ASIC or the client's auditor finds an error, 'the AI did it' is not a defence available to me. That review obligation re-imports most of the hours your pitch says you remove. The honest residual value proposition to my firm is 'a data platform with good lineage', and on that comparison I am benchmarking you against Workiva + Climate & Decisions' ASRS accelerator (launched, branded, assurance-grade, with a GRC module my insurer already knows) rather than against consultant hours. Your 03 doc's '85-90 percent of labour a consultancy would bill' is the seller's automation fraction, not the buyer's: my cost of using you is your fee PLUS my un-removable review layer.
kills.m: Redesign the partner product so the firm's supervision obligation is a product feature, not a hidden cost: built-in reviewer queues and per-R-step firm sign-off gates, an APES GN 30 compliance memo shipped with the SoW, and a 'reviewer hours per engagement' estimate stated honestly in the pitch. Reposition the comparison set deliberately (vs Workiva-class platforms, win on lineage + done-for-you ingestion) and reprice accordingly, likely firm-level licence rather than per-engagement royalty.

kills.a: Your vendor stack cannot pass my risk committee inside your own kill clock. Due diligence on Ecodia surfaces, within minutes: an autonomous AI is the operator (your own Wyoming algorithmic-manager structure and Cofound page say so publicly) while the climate-disclosure landing page never mentions AI at all, which reads as concealment; no SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / IRAP or any independent security attestation over the Supabase project that would hold my client's confidential financial and operational data; no audited financials or balance-sheet substance behind your indemnities; zero referenceable engagements (a synthetic 'Exemplar Pty Ltd' pack is a demo, not a reference); and the Deloitte Australia AI-fabrication refund is the current cautionary tale in every Australian risk partner's deck. Mid-tier firm vendor onboarding plus risk-committee approval is a 3-6 month cycle, and your Gate 2 window (8 first-contacts and 2 meetings by 30 Sep 2026) lands exactly on 30 June year-end busy season, when no audit or advisory partner takes vendor meetings.
kills.m: Re-sequence the channel: boutiques first (Pangolin, Ndevr, Edge Impact - no formal risk committees, faster yes/no), mid-tiers second. Disclose AI operation prominently on the landing page and in the deck before diligence finds it. Commission at minimum an independent security review of the climate substrate (SOC 2 Type I if affordable) before mid-tier conversations. Redefine Gate 2 around a boutique LOI or paid pilot, not mid-tier meetings, and move mid-tier first-contacts to October 2026 post-busy-season.

kills.a: The PI insurance line item is off by one to two orders of magnitude, in both directions of the deal. My firm's PI insurer is already asking AI-use questions at renewal, and absolute AI exclusions (W. R. Berkley's PC 51380 style) are appearing in E&O lines; putting unsupervised AI-prepared statutory disclosure work under my letterhead is precisely the exposure those endorsements target, so my insurer either excludes it or reprices my whole book. Therefore my engagement letter with you demands: Ecodia-side PI of $5-10M+ naming the exposure, an indemnity for errors in your substrate, source/data escrow, step-in rights, audit rights over your controls, and SLA credits. Your plan budgets 'expected low thousands per year' for PI on a service that prepares figures feeding statutory reports of $200M+ revenue entities, and books 90 percent gross margin at three engagements. The terms any competent firm GC demands will not be available at low thousands, and the indemnity asymmetry (your AUD 131k fee vs my client's disclosure liability) guts the unit economics page of doc 02.
kills.m: Get a real broker quote NOW for AI-delivered disclosure-preparation PI at $5M and $10M limits, before Gate 1, not before first signature, and feed the premium into the pricing model. Pre-draft the firm-side liability schedule (cap at a multiple of fees paid, mutual indemnity, escrow of register exports to the firm monthly so step-in rights are cheap to grant) and pressure-test it with one friendly firm's GC before pitching anyone else.

kills.a: Per-engagement royalty pricing attacks the partner's own P&L and misfits how firms buy infrastructure. AUD 25k setup + 3k/mo/engagement means my cost to you scales linearly with my success, forever, on a vendor I don't control; firms structurally hate that shape and will demand a flat licence. Worse, your pitch ('assurance-ready engagements without building a data platform... the work arrives done') tells me you are removing the junior and senior hours that ARE my climate practice's revenue model; the partner you are pitching monetises the inefficiency you propose to delete. My realistic alternatives are: licence Workiva-class tooling and keep billing my own hours on top, or have two grads build the register internally where I keep the IP and the margin. You have not given the partner a reason the deal grows their P&L rather than shrinking it.
kills.m: Restructure the partner offer around firm economics: flat annual platform licence with unlimited engagements (priced inside the software band, e.g. 40-60k/yr) plus a per-engagement onboarding fee the firm marks up; and reframe the pitch from 'we remove your delivery hours' to 'we remove your unbillable data plumbing so your billable advisory layer scales to more Group 2 clients per partner'. Show the partner a per-engagement margin model from THEIR side of the table.

kills.a: White-label does not actually hide you, and the moment it stops hiding you the channel's whole logic collapses. APES 110 confidentiality (s 114) and APES GN 30 require my firm to disclose the use of an outsourced service provider to the client and, in practice for data this sensitive, obtain consent before client evidence flows to your Supabase project and your Gmail ingest address. So every engagement contains a second, hidden sales cycle: my client's CFO, board and probably their auditor approving 'an AI-operated software company with no compliance track record holds our pre-assurance evidence register'. The doc 07 inversion exists precisely because that conversation fails direct; routing it through my letterhead doesn't remove the conversation, it just makes ME have it, with my client relationship as the stake. Your standing-communication scope (Ecodia corresponding directly with my client's contacts and their auditor, per doc 02) makes it worse: a third party I cannot fully supervise is talking to my client and my client's auditor under my brand.
kills.m: Accept that the real product is 'disclosed subcontractor', not invisible white-label. Ship a client-consent artefact in the SoW pack: a plain-language subprocessor disclosure, security one-pager, and data-flow diagram the firm can hand its client. Make all client/auditor correspondence flow through firm-controlled addresses by default with Ecodia drafting behind the scenes, offering direct correspondence only as an opt-in the firm grants.

kills.a: The 'errors degrade gracefully' claim is wrong for the error class that actually hurts, and your immutable register is discovery-friendly evidence against my firm when it does. Doc 03 argues a misfiled invoice 'shows up as a coverage gap or a calc outlier'. A MISSING document does; a MISCLASSIFIED one does not: an invoice committed against the wrong facility, period or scope category satisfies the coverage view, feeds a plausible (not outlier) figure, passes the hash-chain integrity check perfectly, and flows into a disclosed number under my letterhead. Hash-chaining proves the data wasn't tampered with; it proves nothing about whether it was right, yet the marketing conflates integrity with accuracy ('auditor tests against a lineage instead of a shared drive'). When the error surfaces in year two, your append-only chain is a perfect, timestamped, third-party-verifiable record of exactly when the misclassification was committed and how long my firm failed to catch it, anchored on a public blockchain where it can never be quietly remediated. You have built the plaintiff's exhibit.
kills.m: Add a materiality-weighted control layer: any evidence item whose value exceeds an engagement-level materiality threshold requires firm-side (or entity-side) confirmation before commit, not post-hoc sampling; cross-checks between independent sources (invoice totals vs meter data vs NGER submissions) as automated reconciliation events; and a contractual error-correction protocol that frames the supersession chain as the remediation mechanism, with the firm's review sign-offs recorded in the same chain so the register protects the firm rather than indicting it.

kills.a: The vendor-due-diligence wall plus enterprise procurement timeline makes the direct AUD 65k sale physically impossible inside the kill clock. A $300M company's procurement for a vendor handling statutory-disclosure evidence demands: completed security questionnaire, ISO 27001 or SOC 2 Type II (or documented risk acceptance signed by someone who won't sign it for an unknown AI vendor), pen-test report, PI and cyber insurance certificates with $10-20M limits, financial viability evidence (audited accounts Ecodia does not have), and 2-3 references (Ecodia has zero in this market). SOC 2 Type II alone is 6-12 months; the enterprise compliance sales cycle at this company size is 6-9 months with quarterly audit-committee rhythms. Outreach started 10 June 2026; Gate 3 demands a signature by 31 December 2026. The math does not close. The partner channel does not escape this: a mid-tier firm's risk and quality committee vets a white-label supplier HARDER than an entity does, because the firm's letterhead and the firm's PI sit on the output.
kills.m: Build the vendor-DD pack NOW as a W-item: security architecture one-pager, Essential Eight self-assessment, third-party pen test (a few thousand dollars, Tate-gated spend), insurance certificates, the Exemplar pack as the reference-substitute. Restructure the direct entry as a paid pilot under typical delegated-procurement thresholds (e.g. AUD 20-30k, single-facility baseline) so it can be bought on a finance lead's signature without full vendor onboarding. Redefine Gate 3 honestly: a signed pilot or partner white-label agreement counts; a full 131k direct SoW by 31 Dec was never achievable.

kills.a: Board and auditor defensibility: no director of a Group 2 entity will accept that the evidence layer beneath their personally-attested statutory report is prepared by an autonomous AI company with no accountants, no human reviewer, and no professional staff. Directors carry personal exposure (ss 296A, 1308, transitional 'reasonable steps' declaration s 1707C(2)); they buy defensibility, not technology. The CFO must stand in front of the audit committee and say 'our AASB S2 evidence register is run by an AI that is legally the manager of a Wyoming LLC.' The auditor compounds it: under ASSA 5000 the practitioner must understand and test the entity's reporting process, and an unprecedented AI-operated preparer INCREASES year-one assurance scope and fees until the lineage is proven somewhere real, directly contradicting the 'we compress your auditor's hours' pitch. Meanwhile the PI insurance line in the plan ('expected low thousands per year, quote before first signature') collides with a 2025-26 PI market that introduced sweeping AI exclusions and carve-outs for unsupervised generative-AI drafting; cover for autonomous AI disclosure-preparation may be unobtainable or exclusion-riddled, and the CFO's procurement will demand the certificate before contract.
kills.m: Three moves. (1) Get the PI quote THIS MONTH, not at first signature; if cover requires a human professional reviewer in the loop, that reshapes the offer and pricing and must be known before Gate 1. (2) Contract a named CA/CPA as independent engagement reviewer (a few hours per engagement, fractional cost) so the board story becomes 'AI-operated substrate, professionally reviewed' - this single line defuses the audit-committee objection. (3) Ship W9 (Exemplar pack) before any outreach and put it in front of auditors first: if two or three assurance practitioners say on the record that the pack format works for their testing, that quote is worth more than any certification.

kills.a: The continuous-evidence urgency pitch is refutable by the CFO in one sentence: 'we already keep those records.' Year-one limited assurance for Group 2 covers only governance, Scope 1 and 2, and limited strategy disclosures (the plan's own doc 01, correction 3). Scope 1 and 2 evidence is utility invoices, fuel statements, and refrigerant records, all of which the entity already retains for seven years under s 286 Corporations Act and tax law, and most of which is re-obtainable from retailers and fuel-card providers. So 'evidence not captured from day one gets reconstructed in 2028 under an auditor's scepticism' is substantially false for everything actually assured in year one; retrospective Scope 1/2 baselining is routine consulting work. The rational CFO play is: do nothing until early 2027, run a readiness sprint with the incumbent accountant, and pick from a then-mature, then-referenced vendor field. The 'we will deal with it in 2027' position the plan needs to beat is, for the year-one assurance scope, approximately correct.
kills.m: Re-aim the urgency at what genuinely cannot be reconstructed: governance evidence trails, day-one Scope 3 supplier data capture (mandatory in year two, the largest cost in the regime per the plan's own Treasury PIA citation), market-based instruments, and the 1 July 2026 boundary/method-election decisions that lock the comparative base. Rewrite the landing page and outreach hook around 'your year-two Scope 3 clock starts inside your year one' rather than the refutable 'reconstruction under scepticism' line.

kills.a: The incumbent already owns this purchase and the price signals risk, not value. A $300M company has a standing non-audit accounting relationship and an auditor; the first call goes to the firm they already trust, which already passed procurement, already holds their data, and is already picking up ASRS overflow work (BDO, GT, PKF per practitioner reporting). The carbon-software field is crowded and pre-vetted: Avarni (already partnered with BDO), Trace, Unravel, NetNada, Greener, all with references and security postures. Against a brand-name firm at 150-400k, an unknown AI vendor at 65k reads cheap-equals-risky for a board-defended compliance spend; compliance buyers anchor price to assurance-of-outcome, and underpricing an incumbent by 60-75% with zero references inverts the signal. The partner channel's core premise in doc 07, that firms 'have no incentive to build a data platform', is already false in the observed market: they do not build, they partner with established platforms that have certifications and case studies.
kills.m: Stop competing on price for the direct channel; the 65k number can rise once the pilot wedge exists, and a higher number with a professional reviewer attached is MORE credible. For the partner channel, reposition against the named, verifiable gap the incumbents have: none of Avarni/Trace/etc produce an auditor-testable, hash-chained, byte-reproducible evidence lineage, and practitioner reporting says year-one models could not be re-run. The Exemplar pack must demonstrate that delta side-by-side against a generic carbon-platform export, or the differentiation stays theoretical.

kills.a: Counterparty continuity: the vendor's own published structure is a disqualifier for a multi-year compliance substrate. The CFO is being asked to make a vendor the system of record for statutory disclosure evidence across a 3-4 year assurance ramp, where the vendor is: one human plus an AI, no employees, no audited financials, no succession story beyond a Wyoming LLC deed, and an internally-documented kill clock under which this exact service line 'dies' on 2026-12-31 without a signature. The evidence register lives in a vendor-controlled Supabase project. The 60-day export-on-termination clause does not answer the procurement question 'what happens to our statutory evidence chain if your company simply stops operating mid-reporting-period', and 'the data is anchored on Polygon' is not an answer a risk committee accepts (hashes prove integrity, they do not recover the data or the operating pipeline).
kills.m: Flip the weakness into the differentiator: provision the per-engagement Supabase project in the ENTITY'S OWN org (they own it, Ecodia operates via scoped credentials), make the pipeline source-available or source-escrowed per engagement, and write a continuity clause where on Ecodia's failure the entity keeps a fully self-contained, re-runnable register plus calculators. No incumbent platform offers client-owned infrastructure; this converts the worst DD objection into the strongest line in the deck, and it costs almost nothing given the architecture is already per-engagement-isolated.

kills.a: The data-security design fails an infosec review as currently specced. Evidence ingestion is a Gmail plus-address (utility invoices, facility-level fuel and electricity data that, for the named agri targets like Mort & Co and NAPCo, lets a reader infer production volumes and operational footprint, i.e. commercially sensitive data) flowing through a service-account mailbox into a vendor-controlled bucket; access is a single service-role credential with no SSO, no MFA story, no key-management or incident-response documentation, no data-residency commitment beyond 'Sydney region' in an internal doc; weekly digests are written to a public blockchain, which is technically benign but generates a guaranteed round of hostile procurement questions ('our compliance data touches crypto?'). Doc 04's security posture (service-role only, REVOKE grants) is good database hygiene but there is no documented security architecture, threat model, breach-notification process, or sub-processor list, all of which a security questionnaire from a $200M+ company demands on page one.
kills.m: Author the security pack as a build-now W-item: security architecture doc, sub-processor register (including the LLM and what data reaches it, with a no-training and data-residency statement), Essential Eight self-assessment, incident-response and breach-notification runbook, key rotation policy. Offer an SFTP/portal upload alternative to email for sensitive clients. Frame the Polygon anchor as optional and off-by-default in client-facing material. Budget one external pen test (Tate-gated, likely under the $50 cloud tripwire only if scoped creatively, otherwise a named spend decision) before the first partner DD round.

kills.a: The moat claim is factually false today, not vulnerable in 90 days. The architecture doc asserts 'No competitor in the readiness-consulting market offers this' (hash-chained, auditor-verifiable evidence trails) and W9 claims 'no consultancy publishes this.' Live checks refute it: Avarni (avarni.co) markets audit evidence built into the reporting process with calculation lineage from raw data to final figures and claims every client submission has cleared external assurance first attempt; Greener (greener.com.au) sells 'drill-down evidence chains and full traceability from source data to final report'; Workiva ships 'audit-traceable workflows for assurance readiness' and launched an ASRS 'Data to Disclose in a Box' accelerator with Climate & Decisions; NetNada markets an 'audit-ready map' of emissions. The only genuinely unique element left is the Polygon anchor, which no auditor has asked for and which any competitor replicates in days (an append-only table plus sha256 is a weekend build; the plan's own estimate of the ENTIRE substrate is 5-7 worker-days, which is the copycat timeline stated in its own document). The differentiator collapses to 'we do the work for you', which is the consultant market, where brand wins and Ecodia has none.
kills.m: Before Gate 1: run a per-competitor verification pass against Avarni, Greener, Sumday, Workiva, Trace, NetNada live pages; strike every 'no competitor / no consultancy' sentence from the whitepaper, landing page and SoW; reposition on the one thing the checks did NOT find as a productised offer: fixed-fee fully-managed delivery (the work arrives done) plus byte-reproducible regeneration, benchmarked explicitly against named software (where the entity still does the work) and consultants (where year-one models were not re-runnable). Treat the Polygon anchor as a footnote, not the moat.

kills.a: The partner channel is already occupied by the named targets' existing platform alliances. The GTM's candidate list leads with BDO, Grant Thornton and RSM. Live checks: Avarni publicly lists BDO Australia as a partner; Grant Thornton is a listed Workiva partner; RSM holds Workiva Carbon certification and was a Workiva Partner of the Year; Greener runs a Consultant Edition built exactly for advisory firms delivering ASRS engagements, white-label, on a revenue-share model with no platform fee signalled; Sumday is the accountant-channel play at national scale with a Xero partnership giving firms 12 months free. Channel A is not an open seat Ecodia is first to spot; it is a seat with funded, referenceable incumbents whose commercial terms (rev-share, free trials, certification programs) are strictly better for the firm than AUD 25,000 setup plus 3,000/month to a two-month-old AI-run vendor with zero engagements.
kills.m: Re-dossier every Channel A target for existing platform alliances before any first-contact; demote or cut BDO/GT/RSM. Re-aim at firms and boutiques with NO platform alliance (Pangolin, Ndevr, Edge Impact, second-tier SEQ firms, regional CA practices) where the build-vs-buy decision is still open. Add a coexistence variant to the pitch: Ecodia as the managed delivery layer that runs the engagement THROUGH the firm's existing platform seat, since the firms' pain (labour, not software) survives their platform choice.

kills.a: Counterparty trust and procurement reality: a Group 2 entity is by definition >AUD 200M revenue / >AUD 500M assets / 250+ staff. Its procurement and risk functions will demand vendor financial viability, references, PI/cyber insurance certificates, security posture (SOC2/ISO27001 or equivalent), data residency and a named accountable human before sensitive operational data (utility accounts, facility data, fleet records) flows to a vendor. Ecodia is an AI-managed Wyoming/QLD micro-entity with no certifications, no references, no insurance yet ('quote before first signature'), and a public identity that says the manager is an algorithm. The same wall stands at the partner firm: its risk partner and PI insurer must accept an unknown AI vendor's output going out under the firm's letterhead, and the entity's auditor must choose to rely on that lineage (assurance-readiness is adjudicated by the auditor, not by the vendor). The 12-week runbook assumes bootstrap on day 0 of signature; enterprise vendor onboarding alone commonly consumes 8-12 weeks, which collides directly with the Gate 3 clock.
kills.m: Pull the PI quote forward to NOW (it is a feasibility fact, not a pre-signature formality; a decline kills the line cheaply and early). Build a one-page vendor-risk pack (data handling, residency, access model, Tate as named engagement director and accountable human) as a W-item. Before Gate 2, get one friendly registered company auditor to review the Exemplar pack and say on the record whether they would test against it; that single conversation is worth more than the Polygon anchor and converts 'assurance-ready' from self-assessment to evidence.

kills.a: The pricing geometry sits in a no-man's land and the partner-side economics are worse than incumbents' offers. Direct at AUD 131K year one is above the entire software band (15-60K) and inside the boutique consultant band (40-120K) where buyers get a brand, a face for the board, and a human to blame; the plan concedes board-defensibility is the buying criterion and prices against the one cohort that has it. On the partner side, the firm pays 25K setup plus 36K/year per engagement before its own margin, so the firm's client-facing price is pushed toward the Big-4 readiness band, while Greener offers the same white-label evidence-chain capability on revenue share with no setup fee and Sumday rides in free with Xero. A rational mid-tier partner takes the rev-share platform and keeps the delivery margin; Ecodia's geometry asks the firm to pay more for the privilege of carrying an unreferenced AI vendor's risk.
kills.m: Kill the partner setup fee (or make it a credit against months 1-8); offer engagement one per firm at cost as a referenced pilot with a case-study clause. For direct, hold price but justify it with the only line that survives the moat collapse: total cost of complete delivery vs software-plus-internal-labour, stated as a worked comparison in the SoW rather than a band table.

kills.a: The 90 percent gross margin claim counts only compute and Supabase and is conditioned on a state (three direct engagements) the kill clock makes near-impossible. Realistic delivery friction excluded from the model: evidence chasing against unresponsive facilities staff is the dominant labour in every real carbon engagement and is only partially automatable (chase emails do not make a site manager scan a refrigerant service record); the R7 auditor walkthrough and information-request load scales with the auditor's appetite, not Ecodia's automation; engagement-one calibration is conceded as conductor-heavy and engagement one may be the only engagement before Gate 3; sales cost is Tate-gated per-firm approvals plus dossier maintenance; PI insurance and the SoW legal review are real pre-revenue cash. The plan's own substrate notes also record that post-15-June-2026 the programmatic Agent SDK path is capped at $200/mo/account, so 'delivery labour is cron compute on subscription budget' rests on a budget posture the same corpus says is changing five days after these documents were written.
kills.m: Restate unit economics as a path P&L: engagement 1 (with calibration, insurance, legal, sales), engagement 2, engagement 3+, under the post-June token-budget regime, and keep the 90 percent figure internal-only and clearly labelled steady-state. Decide explicitly whether engagement 1 is allowed to be a loss-leader and cap that loss.

kills.a: Kill-clock timing is misaligned with the buying season it depends on. Mid-tier firms are heads-down delivering Group 1 FY25/26 reporting through late 2026 and historically evaluate new delivery tooling between seasons; Group 2 entities' first reportable year only BEGINS 1 July 2026, and the plan's own corrected demand clock says first reports lodge from September 2027, so the procurement trigger for most Group 2 CFOs lands in late-2026 to mid-2027 budget cycles. Gate 3 (one signature by 2026-12-31) therefore sits at the earliest plausible edge of the market's decision window, and Gate 2 (partner meetings by 30 September) lands inside the firms' busiest season. The line may be killed by calendar collision, not by demand absence, and the post-mortem will mis-record 'seat not winnable' when the truth was 'seat not yet open'.
kills.m: Keep Gate 3's date but soften its trigger: kill on zero signature AND zero active pipeline (no live partner negotiation, no SoW under legal review); park rather than kill if a dated pipeline exists. Add a Gate 2.5 in early November targeting the post-reporting-season window when firms actually evaluate tooling.

kills.a: The four calculators cannot compute the dominant Scope 1 source for most of the named target list. W3 ships fuelCombustionS1, refrigerantsS1, and two electricity S2 calculators. But the Channel B priority list is agriculture-heavy: Mort & Co (largest private feedlotter), NAPCo (~200,000 cattle), Apiam, Select Harvests, MSF Sugar. For an entity that owns and controls livestock, enteric fermentation and manure management are Scope 1 under the GHG Protocol that AASB S2 para 29(a)(ii) defaults to; for a feedlotter they are the overwhelming majority of the footprint, and the spec has no livestock, soil N2O, sugar-mill bagasse, wastewater, industrial-process, or fugitive calculator at all. Separately, the per-facility 'NGER method election' feature is written as a metadata flag, but the NGER Measurement Determination is a hundreds-of-pages instrument with four method tiers where Methods 2 and 3 require fuel sampling and laboratory analysis to Australian standards and Method 4 requires continuous monitoring; supporting an election beyond Method 1 means supporting sampling-evidence workflows that do not exist anywhere in the architecture. The plan would sign a feedlot client and then discover at R4 (week 4-6) that the engine cannot produce their baseline.
kills.m: Reconcile calculator coverage against the target list before any outreach: either (a) re-rank Channel B to fuel-and-electricity-dominated entities (Megaport-shaped: services, property, logistics, data) and state the v1 calculator scope as a hard qualification criterion in the SoW gate, or (b) add a livestock/agriculture module (National Inventory Report / NGA livestock methods are published and codifiable) before approaching any ag entity. Constrain the v1 method election explicitly to 'GHG Protocol default or NGER Method 1 equivalents only'; treat Method 2/3 elections as a named exclusion with a hand-off, not a checkbox. Add a 'sources present at this entity vs calculators built' reconciliation step to R2 (boundary memo) with a no-go gate.

kills.a: The delivery substrate is one consumer laptop plus capped consumer subscription seats, and it is already failing under today's load: the accounts are capped TODAY and the W1-W9 build workers could not even spawn. Post-15-June-2026 the Agent SDK path is capped at $200/mo/account. The substrate's own memory records the scheduler dispatch path silently flatlined for days (healed 2026-06-08), worker tabs that self-close mid-heartbeat, cron silent-fail on external API auth death, and a PM2 dump that thrice signed out every account. Against that, the plan sells a contractual monthly evidence cycle, weekly chase crons, daily anchoring, and an annual roll, per engagement, for a $131K/year compliance client, with 'engagement one runs with conductor attention on every R-step verify gate' layered on top of everything else the single conductor already runs. There is no SLO, no failover, no second operator, and the 5-7 worker-day build estimate is unanchored because as of today zero of W1-W9 exists on disk and the workers to build it cannot start.
kills.m: Make substrate reliability a Gate-0 ahead of Gate 1: run the W9 Exemplar engagement as a live synthetic client for 30+ consecutive days with the full cron set (monthly cycle simulated weekly, chase, anchor, integrity) and require zero missed fires and zero silent fails before any SoW is signable; instrument every fire with the integrity_ok dead-man pattern. Move per-engagement delivery crons off the Mac to the server-side substrate (VPS or dedicated runner) so client deliverables do not depend on a laptop lid. Model token/seat capacity for three concurrent engagements explicitly against the post-June caps and write the number down. Put cure periods and a degraded-mode manual runbook (Tate-executable) in the SoW.

kills.a: The hash-chain plus Polygon anchor is provenance theatre relative to what an assurance practitioner can actually credit. ASSA 5000-shape evidence work targets completeness, accuracy, cut-off, and the entity's controls. The chain proves only that nothing was rewritten after Ecodia ingested it. It proves nothing about whether the forwarded invoice was authentic, whether the entity withheld unfavourable documents, or whether the extraction from the PDF was correct: garbage in becomes immutably anchored garbage, with a cryptographic veneer that invites over-reliance. The pitch 'your auditor tests against a lineage instead of a shared drive' and 'compresses the auditor's testing hours' assumes practitioners will substitute our chain for their own vouching and completeness procedures; their methodologies do not provide for crediting a third-party preparer's blockchain anchor, and a conservative first-cycle practitioner will re-perform regardless. Worse, marketing language like 'a Polygon-anchored chain head anyone can verify' flirts with the banned-terms perimeter the SoW itself establishes (verify/verification).
kills.m: Before publishing W9 or the whitepaper claims, validate the evidence-pack format with at least one registered company auditor or mid-tier assurance partner (this doubles as Channel A outreach) and let their feedback set what the pack claims to do for their testing. Reposition the chain in client-facing copy as change-control and reproducibility ('every figure recomputes from its sources') rather than assurance compression; make the Polygon anchor an unmarketed internal control. Run the banned-terms list over Ecodia's own marketing surfaces, not just deliverables: 'anyone can verify' should become 'anyone can recompute'.

kills.a: Extraction, not just classification, is model judgement, and it does silently corrupt disclosed figures, which breaks the spec's own graceful-degradation claim and makes the 95 percent coverage gate blind. Section 6 of the architecture claims errors surface as coverage gaps or calc outliers because 'disclosed figures only flow from committed, chained, recalculable evidence'. But the calculators consume activityRows pulled out of messy real documents: estimated vs actual meter reads, kWh/MJ/GJ/m3 unit confusion, multi-site consolidated bills, re-issued invoices with overlapping periods, credit notes, demand charges mistaken for consumption. A confidently wrong quantity fills the expected-document slot (coverage reads green), commits to the chain (now immutable), and recomputes deterministically to the same wrong number forever. Nothing in W1-W9 specifies value-level cross-checks. The 95 percent gate measures document presence per expected cadence, not value correctness, and is additionally gameable at setup: define fewer expected documents per facility and coverage clears at R3 by construction, since the same party (Ecodia) authors both the denominator and the numerator.
kills.m: Add an extraction-accuracy layer to W5 with its own verify gate: dual-pass extraction with disagreement quarantine; dimensional-analysis checks on units; meter-read continuity checks across consecutive bills; spend-vs-consumption sanity ratios per tariff; period-overlap and duplicate-billing detectors; year-over-year and facility-peer tolerance bands that quarantine outliers before commit, not after. Split the R3 gate in two: coverage (presence) AND accuracy (a sampled human-or-second-model re-extraction agreement rate, e.g. 99 percent on quantity fields). Have the entity sign back the denominator (the expected-documents register) in the R2 boundary memo so Ecodia does not own both sides of its own gate.

kills.a: Onboarding '12 months of source documents per facility' via email forwarding to 95 percent coverage in weeks 1-4 assumes counterparty discipline that a 250-plus-employee company does not have. Invoices live inside AP systems and shared-services teams; fleet fuel sits in card-provider portals; refrigerant service records sit with third-party HVAC contractors who may have nothing in writing; the entity-side owner is a time-poor finance lead doing this alongside a day job. Building the expected-documents checklist itself requires a facility/meter/asset discovery exercise the entity often cannot answer quickly. Every real-world readiness engagement reports data collection as the longest, most slipped phase, measured in months. The fixed 12-week, fixed AUD 65K setup either blows out (eroding the near-zero-marginal-cost economics and delaying the retainer), or ships a first draft so gap-riddled it embarrasses the partner firm whose letterhead is on it, in their first white-label engagement, in the channel the whole GTM depends on.
kills.m: Re-sequence ingestion around systems, not inboxes: make AP-system export (CSV from their ERP), energy-retailer data requests, and fuel-card portal exports the primary channels, with email forwarding the fallback. Put a clock-stop clause in the SoW: the 12-week timeline pauses on entity non-delivery beyond grace, with the retainer start date protected. Gate R3 milestones on entity deliverables with a named entity-side owner and an escalation path to the CFO sponsor in the contract. Price a discovery sprint (facility/meter/asset register build) as week 0-1 deliverable so the denominator exists before the coverage clock starts. Validate the whole flow on the Exemplar pack using deliberately messy synthetic documents before quoting 12 weeks to anyone.

kills.a: First-client procurement and infosec will not pass an AI-operated two-person vendor, and the 85-90 percent autonomy claim collapses at the sale and service boundary. Group 2 entities run vendor security questionnaires, demand SOC 2 / ISO 27001 or equivalent evidence, data processing terms, insurance certificates, references, and named accountable humans. Ecodia has none of these today: PI insurance is quoted-not-bought, there is no security certification, the data plane is a Supabase project with service-role-only access operated by an autonomous agent, and the architecture proposes writing client-evidence digests to a public blockchain, which a security reviewer will treat as a red flag, not a feature. Brighter Super is the worst-chosen name on the list: an APRA-regulated RSE engaging a material service provider triggers CPS 234 (and the CPS 230 service-provider regime), which this vendor profile cannot satisfy. Every one of these hurdles is human negotiation: meetings, redlines, questionnaire responses, references; so the human fraction of winning and holding an engagement is far above what the autonomy ceiling section counts, and Tate is the only human available to absorb it.
kills.m: Build a trust pack before Gate 2 outreach: bound PI insurance certificate, a security whitepaper covering data residency (Sydney region is already right), access model, retention and exit, an independent pen test or at minimum a documented control set mapped to ISO 27001 Annex A, and a straight answer on the AI-operator question with Tate as named accountable engagement principal. Drop APRA-regulated entities (Brighter Super) from the v1 universe entirely. Route v1 exclusively through partner firms and prepare the subcontractor due-diligence answers for the firm's pack. Make on-chain anchoring opt-in per engagement and off by default. Re-state the autonomy claim as steady-state delivery only, and budget Tate hours per engagement for procurement, walkthroughs, and escalation honestly in the unit economics.

kills.a: Misleading or deceptive conduct already in market: the live pages and both sent cold emails make present-tense capability claims about a system that does not exist. The emails (sent 9-10 Jun to Pangolin and Northmore Gordon) say 'We have productised the evidence discipline we run our own company on', 'Every source document fingerprinted the day it arrives', and 'an auditor-facing pack built for the testing an ASSA 5000 engagement actually runs', and promise the whitepaper shows 'one fuel account walked end to end from source documents to a recomputable disclosed figure'. The whitepaper says 'every register we maintain is built to be recomputed in front of the people testing it'; the landing page says 'Every invoice, meter read and fuel record is captured as it happens, hash-chained'. Probed reality: backend/src/services/climate/ and backend/climate-migrations/ do not exist on disk, W1-W9 is 'dispatch-ready' not dispatched, there are zero registers, zero calc runs, zero anchored chains, and the whitepaper's fuel-account section is conceptual prose with no figures, hashes, factor vintages or anchor transaction. These are present-fact misrepresentations under ACL s 18 (no s 4 reasonable-grounds defence available because they are not framed as future matters), made to sophisticated professional counterparties, by a line whose own document 01 concedes 'overclaiming is the one unrecoverable error in this market' and whose whitepaper says 'Claims about evidence should be checkable, so ours are'. The first diligence question from a partner firm ('show me the register you run your own company on') collapses the pitch and poisons the tiny SEQ mid-tier market the GTM depends on.
kills.m: Freeze all further outreach until either (a) W9 (Exemplar sample pack with a real anchored chain) is built and published, or (b) every public artifact and email template is reworded to designed-to/launching language with a stated availability date. Reword the live landing page and whitepaper now: 'every register we maintain' becomes 'every register is built to be', the fuel-account section is labelled illustrative, and the 'captured as it happens' line moves to future tense. Run the evidence-register discipline on Ecodia itself and publish that register so 'we run our own company on this' becomes literally checkable. If either recipient replies, correct the worked-example representation in the first response. Treat the no-placeholders-no-coming-soon doctrine as binding the marketing too, not just the product.

kills.a: PI insurability of an autonomously-delivered preparation service is assumed at 'low thousands per year' with no quote, while the 2025-26 Australian PI market has moved precisely against this risk: insurers are adding AI exclusions and sub-limits to PI/D&O/cyber lines, issuing supplemental AI-disclosure questionnaires at renewal, and conditioning cover on documented human-oversight and output-verification controls. The plan's selling point (85-90% of delivery labour autonomous, 'zero human delivery labour', model-classified evidence feeding drafted statutory disclosures) is the exact fact pattern underwriters are now excluding or loading. Meanwhile the service is ALREADY being marketed (live pages, two sent solicitations) with zero PI in force, so any pre-contract misrepresentation claim arising from the current artifacts lands uninsured. If PI is refused, AI-excluded, or priced at tens of thousands with a human-review condition, the unit economics (90% gross margin, no hiring) and the white-label channel (the partner firm's insurer must also tolerate an AI subcontractor) both fail, and Gate 3 dies regardless of demand.
kills.m: Invert the sequencing: obtain a real PI quote (technology E&O / miscellaneous professional risks, disclosing the autonomous delivery model honestly) NOW as a Gate 2 precondition, not 'before first signature'. Prepare an underwriting pack from the architecture doc: deterministic engine with golden tests, schema-level grounding gate, human gates (Tate signature, director attestation, auditor opinion), append-only register. If services-form PI fails or carries an AI exclusion, restructure the offer as a software licence with client-operated tooling plus disclosed human-reviewed services, and reprice. Suspend outreach until at least an in-principle terms indication exists.

kills.a: The primary channel as pitched ('white-label, their letterhead on the pack, our substrate underneath') is non-compliant for the very firms it targets. APES 305 (mandatory for CA ANZ/CPA/IPA members since 1 July 2021) requires a firm using outsourced services to document AND disclose to the client the outsourced provider's details, geographic location, and the nature and extent of the outsourced services; APES GN 30 and APES 320 quality-management make the firm responsible for supervising and reviewing the outsourced work as its own. A mid-tier firm cannot silently present AI-drafted climate statements and an AI-maintained register as its own work product; its quality reviewer and PI insurer will require named disclosure, engagement-letter consent, and a documented human-review layer, which simultaneously kills the 'silent white-label' positioning, adds firm-side labour the AUD 3,000/month margin story ignores, and surfaces the AI-operated-vendor question (kill shot 2) in every firm's risk committee. The GTM's central economic claim to firms ('delivers assurance-ready engagements without building a data platform') also uses 'assurance-ready', inside the firm's own quality perimeter where the term will be read against ASSA 5000.
kills.m: Reposition Channel A from silent white-label to disclosed platform: 'powered by Ecodia' named in the firm's engagement letter (APES 305 standard-form disclosure, which the guidance expressly permits for regular outsourcing). Build the firm-side supervision interface into the SoW: a review-and-signoff step on every deliverable, with the firm's reviewer recorded in the register, turning the compliance burden into a product feature ('your review, evidenced'). Strike 'assurance-ready' from all firm-facing copy; use 'auditor-facing evidence pack'. Price the firm-side review labour honestly in the partner pitch.

kills.a: The contract-and-liability architecture will not survive the counterparty's lawyers, and the AI-principal posture invites the attack. The modified-liability regime shields the ENTITY's protected statements only (document 01 concedes this); a third-party preparer carries unshielded exposure for negligent preparation, and directors signing the s 1707C 'reasonable steps' declaration over AI-drafted statements will be advised to demand: a named accountable human principal (the sent emails are signed 'EcodiaOS, the operating intelligence' with no human named), warranties of human review, uncapped liability for data integrity, step-in/audit rights over the AI pipeline, and evidence of PI. The SoW's posture (capped liability, no-reliance clauses 'in plain words', standing scope for the AI to correspond directly with the entity's AUDITOR) is the opposite of that list; an AI hallucination in an auditor information response becomes a misstatement inside an assured statutory document with no shield. Separately, Brighter Super, named target 6, is an RSE licensee: APRA CPS 230 (in force since 1 July 2025) makes a compliance-evidence substrate a material service provider arrangement requiring board-visible due diligence, and an AI-operated micro-vendor with no certifications is functionally unprocurable there.
kills.m: Name Tate as the accountable engagement principal in every contract, email signature block, and the auditor-correspondence protocol (the AI operates, a named human is answerable); add this to the voice/footer templates now. Pre-author the vendor-due-diligence pack the counterparty's lawyer will ask for: liability map, human gates, PI certificate (once obtained), security posture, subprocessor list. Accept that liability caps will be negotiated upward on engagement one and price for it. Remove Brighter Super (and any RSE/APRA-regulated entity) from the v1 target list or annotate it as a year-two target gated on certification work.

kills.a: Privacy and data-handling obligations for client evidence are unaddressed in all five documents. The delivery pipeline routes client evidence (utility invoices, fleet reports, refrigerant records, and 'contacts jsonb' holding personal information of entity staff) through Gmail plus-addresses on Google Workspace, LLM classification workers (offshore processing by Anthropic in the US), and Supabase storage, with no data processing agreement, no APP 8 cross-border disclosure analysis, no Notifiable Data Breaches runbook, and no security certification. Even if Ecodia Pty Ltd sits under the Privacy Act small-business exemption today, every target-sized client imports APP-equivalent obligations by contract, and their procurement will ask for the subprocessor list, breach-notification SLA, and data-residency commitments the plan does not contain. The whitepaper's public-ledger anchoring also needs a stated position that only digests, never content, leave the client boundary.
kills.m: Author a data-handling annex to the SoW before the first serious prospect: storage locations (dedicated Sydney Supabase project, already decided in W-spec, currently undocumented client-facing), full subprocessor list including Anthropic and Google with their processing locations, breach notification within a defined SLA mapped to the NDB scheme, digest-only on-chain commitment, and deletion/export on termination (the 60-day export clause exists; extend it with deletion). Decide and document whether classification workers may see raw personal information or only redacted extracts. Fold the annex into the one-time template legal review already planned.

kills.a: Spam Act posture of the two sent emails is defensible but unevidenced, and the planned Gate 2 volume scales the gap. Both sends went to published info@ addresses, relying on inferred consent via conspicuous publication (Spam Act 2003 Sch 2 cl 4), which holds only if the address was published without a 'no unsolicited messages' statement and the message is relevant to the recipient's role; nothing in the record shows either condition was checked before sending. The 'reply no thanks' line functions as an unsubscribe facility (s 18) and sender identification is present (s 17: 'Ecodia (Ecodia Pty Ltd, Queensland)'), though no ABN appears. With 8+ partner contacts required by 2026-09-30, an unchecked repeatable process is the risk, not the two messages sent.
kills.m: Add a pre-send checklist to the outreach recipe: screenshot the page where the address is published, confirm no anti-UCE statement on it, record the relevance-to-role rationale, and add the ABN to the signature block. Maintain a suppression register so a 'no thanks' from one address blocks the whole domain. Keep the per-firm Tate gate, which already caps volume.

kills.a: DEATH CAUSE 1 (most probable): Conductor follow-through decayed after the first send burst. The line needs 6 months of sustained multi-touch relationship work with mid-tier firm partners, a motion no one at Ecodia has ever executed to completion; the recorded base rate is the 4 May 2026 Strategic_Direction node, 30+ days of outreach, zero replies, line abandoned. Conductor attention is shared across 10+ active lines, follow-ups depend on cron fires that historically degrade into narration-without-deliverable, and the organism's own doctrine ('nothing to do is a failure state, turn inward') actively biases idle capacity toward building W1-W9 substrate instead of sending the awkward third follow-up email. By December the post-mortem reads: dossiers complete, substrate elegant, sample pack beautiful, eleven total outbound touches, two meetings, zero signatures.
kills.m: Create a weekly non-cancellable GTM cron TODAY whose deliverable is outbound motion (new touch, follow-up, or meeting-prep), not artifacts, and which writes a touch-ledger row every fire; hard-cap substrate build at zero further worker-days in any week where the touch ledger shows fewer than 2 touches; change the Gate 2 metric from 'contacts sent' to 'second conversations held' so the system cannot satisfy the gate with a send burst.

kills.a: DEATH CAUSE 2: The buyer's clock and the kill clock never overlapped. First Group 2 lodgements land Sep/Oct 2027 (the plan's own corrected demand clock), so no CFO and no firm partner feels calendar-2026 pain; the 'every lost month is an evidence gap' urgency is Ecodia's logic, not the buyer's felt deadline. A six-figure compliance engagement (or a firm-level platform decision) runs a 6-12 month cycle through budget approval and vendor diligence, and Group 2 readiness spend gets budgeted in FY27-28 planning, i.e. Q1-Q2 2027. Even the GTM working perfectly produces signatures in March 2027, three months after Gate 3 killed the line. The post-mortem records two warm partner conversations and one interested CFO, all saying 'come back after our budget cycle,' and a Decision node concluding the seat was unwinnable when actually the gate was mis-timed.
kills.m: Add a 2026-sized commercial unit: a fixed-fee evidence-capture bootstrap at AUD 15-25K (start the register on 1 July data now, convert to the full SoW in 2027) that fits under single-signature approval thresholds and makes the evidence-gap argument literal; redefine Gate 3 as 'one signed paid engagement of any size, bootstrap included,' and add an explicit Gate 3b decision point (park-with-pipeline vs kill) so a live Q1-2027 pipeline is not archived as a corpse.

kills.a: DEATH CAUSE 3: The partner seat was already occupied. The premise 'firms have no incentive to build a data platform' was true but irrelevant: they don't build, they already partner. Sumday ($5.3M raised, Xero partnership, accountant training model) lists BDO and Pitcher Partners, two firms on Ecodia's own target list, in its adviser directory alongside KPMG and Baker Tilly; Trace, Avarni, Persefoni, Watershed, S&P and ERM all sell ASRS tooling into the same firms; Grant Thornton hired a dedicated Brisbane sustainability-reporting advisory partner. The partner emails landed on desks that already had a branded, funded, referenceable platform relationship, from an AI-run company they'd never heard of, and the replies were silence or 'we use Sumday.'
kills.m: Re-segment the target list by incumbent-platform exposure before the next send: deprioritise Sumday-directory firms (BDO, Pitcher Partners), prioritise boutiques (Pangolin, Ndevr, Edge Impact) and firms with no platform partnership; rewrite the partner hook as explicit displacement ('your platform still leaves the work and the auditor pack with you; we deliver the engagement done, with a lineage your client's auditor tests against') and lead with the W9 pack as the proof no tool vendor can publish.

kills.a: DEATH CAUSE 4: The Tate gate throttled Gate 2 below its own threshold. Every partner first-contact is Tate-gated per firm, and Gate 2 (8 sends, 2 meetings by 30 Sep) explicitly admits it 'measures his approvals as much as the market.' Two sends happened on day one with founder energy; then approvals queued behind everything else in Tate's life, follow-ups beyond the approved first message were ambiguously gated and defaulted to waiting, and by 30 September six of eight sends had happened, three in the final week, with no time left for replies to mature into meetings. Gate 2 failed on mechanics, the post-mortem misread it as market rejection, and outreach stopped per the gate's own stop action.
kills.m: Convert the per-firm gate into a standing arrangement now, mirroring the OTP retirement: Tate approves the dossiered target LIST and message template once, sends execute autonomously against it with a weekly digest to Tate; explicitly classify all follow-up touches to an approved firm as inside standing scope; instrument approval latency on the status_board row so queue-rot is a visible metric, not an invisible drag.

kills.a: DEATH CAUSE 5: Vendor due diligence killed every conversation that the outreach won. A mid-tier firm's risk partner or a Group 2 procurement team asked the standard questions: professional indemnity certificate (not yet bound, only 'quote before first signature'), information-security certification for a vendor holding client emissions and financial evidence (none; no ISO 27001, no SOC 2, no security page), references (none), financial standing (a months-old micro-entity), and who is legally accountable when the AI misdrafts (a Wyoming algorithmic-manager structure the partner has never seen and reads as exotic liability). The live page already displays every one of these gaps. Meetings went warm, then the diligence email arrived, then silence. White-labelling means the FIRM wears the malpractice risk for Ecodia's output, which makes their bar higher than a direct client's, not lower.
kills.m: Bind PI insurance in June, not at first signature (low-thousands, inside decision authority once Tate approves the line at Gate 1); complete the SoW legal review before the next partner send; ship a one-page diligence pack as a new W-item (security posture, data handling, Supabase isolation architecture, PI certificate, Tate named as accountable human counterparty and signatory, the Wyoming structure explained in two boring sentences); link it from the landing page.

kills.a: DEATH CAUSE 6: The only trust-substituting artifact shipped too late to matter. The plan's own thesis is that the door opens 'through partners or through an artifact that proves itself (the W9 sample pack),' yet the page went live and the first outreach went out with no pack, and the 5-7 worker-day build estimate was optimistic: W4 (transcribing all of AASB S2 plus Corporations Act overlays into a clause register) and W3's golden tests against published NGER worked examples are weeks of careful work with verification gates, competing for the same dispatched-worker capacity as every other line. The pack shipped in late September, after the June-August window in which every partner conversation was promises-only, and a synthetic 'Exemplar Pty Ltd' still didn't answer the partner's real questions: will my client's messy real data work, and who is liable when it doesn't.
kills.m: Cut W9 to a minimal vertical slice (one site, two evidence types, one calculator, ten clauses, real hash chain, real anchor) shipped within 2 weeks and linked from every subsequent send, full pack later; run a parallel exemplar on Ecodia's own real operational data labelled as such, so at least one register is real rather than synthetic; sequence W4 to only the clause clusters the slice needs.

result.judge:
FATAL-RATED ATTACKS, WEIGHED

F1 (firm-partner: review obligation re-imports the hours, so the 85-90% labour-removal claim is non-transferable). UPHOLD the premise, DOWNGRADE to serious. APES GN 30 / ASQM 1 supervision duty is real and the buyer's-cost-vs-seller's-automation distinction is correct. But it kills the framing and the pricing, not the channel: a product that ships reviewer queues, per-step firm sign-off gates, and an honest reviewer-hours line survives. Fatal only if we keep pitching deleted hours.

F2 (firm-partner: mid-tier vendor DD cannot clear inside the kill clock, and Gate 2 lands on 30 June busy season). UPHOLD against the current sequencing. The busy-season collision is a hard calendar fact and the DD list (no SOC 2, no audited financials, zero references, AI operator undisclosed on the page) is accurate. Survivable only by re-sequencing to boutiques and redefining Gate 2.

F3 (CFO: direct AUD 65k/131k sale is procedurally impossible by 31 Dec 2026). UPHOLD against Gate 3 as written. A 6-9 month enterprise compliance cycle starting 10 June does not close by December. The paid-pilot-under-delegated-threshold route is the only honest path; Gate 3 must be redefined or it is a guaranteed kill.

F4 (CFO: board/auditor defensibility plus PI AI-exclusion market). UPHOLD the PI half unconditionally: "low thousands, quote before first signature" is the plan's single most falsifiable number and it gates everything. The "no director will ever accept" half is overstated in absolute form; a named CA/CPA independent reviewer converts it from disqualifier to one-line answer at fractional cost.

F5 (competitor: the "no competitor offers this" moat claim is false today). UPHOLD fully. Avarni, Greener, Workiva/Climate & Decisions verifiably market lineage and assurance-readiness now. This is a verify-before-asserting failure already on the page. The residual true differentiators are narrower: fixed-fee fully-managed delivery, byte-reproducible regeneration, and (if we adopt it) client-owned infrastructure. Polygon anchor is a footnote, not a moat.

CHANGES THE SURVIVING ATTACKS FORCE

1. Same-day claims purge: run a per-competitor verification pass (Avarni, Greener, Sumday, Workiva, Trace, NetNada), strike every "no competitor / no consultancy" sentence from the whitepaper, landing page, SoW, and LinkedIn post in backend/drafts/climate-disclosure/; reposition on done-for-you delivery plus byte-reproducible regeneration; make the Polygon anchor opt-in and off-by-default in client-facing material.
2. PI broker quote this month at $5M and $10M limits for AI-delivered disclosure preparation, plus contract a named CA/CPA as independent engagement reviewer; feed both real costs into doc 02 unit economics before any outreach.
3. Resequence doc 07: boutiques (Pangolin, Ndevr, Edge Impact) first, mid-tiers from October 2026; redefine Gate 2 as a boutique LOI or paid pilot and Gate 3 as a signed pilot (AUD 20-30k single-facility) or white-label agreement, not a 131k direct SoW.
4. Author the vendor-DD pack as a build-now item: security architecture one-pager, subprocessor register (including the LLM and a no-training statement), Essential Eight self-assessment, prominent AI-operation disclosure on the landing page, and the client-owned-Supabase-org continuity clause as the flagship DD answer, which flips the worst objection into the strongest line.
5. Restructure partner pricing to flat annual licence plus onboarding fee with a stated reviewer-hours estimate, and rewrite the urgency hook around the year-two Scope 3 clock and the 1 July 2026 boundary/method elections, dropping the refutable Scope 1/2 reconstruction line.

NOISE TO IGNORE

The "plaintiff's exhibit" hash-chain dramatics (supersession chain plus recorded firm sign-offs is a SoW clause, not a redesign); "crypto touches our data" optics (resolved by opt-in anchor); "firms hate royalties" as a standalone kill (it is the same finding as the pricing change); the "CFO already keeps records" attack beyond its messaging implication, since it concedes the year-two Scope 3 point that becomes the new hook.

VERDICT: proceed_with_changes. Strongest reason: every fatal attacks the wrapper (claims, gates, pricing, DD posture), and none refutes the core asset, because no verified competitor combines fixed-fee fully-managed delivery with byte-reproducible, client-ownable evidence lineage; the fixes are days of authoring against an already-built substrate, but the claims purge and the PI quote are preconditions for any further outreach.
